claims.text: The Nevada Triangle, a ~25,000 square-mile region of the Sierra Nevada spanning Las Vegas, Fresno, and Reno, has claimed approximately 2,000 aircraft in the past 60 years.

evidence.quote:
In the past 60 years, the Nevada
triangle has claimed two thousand aircraft.

claims.rationale: The 2,000-crash figure is widely repeated online but traces back to the Nevada Aerospace Museum/NTSB totals for the broader Sierra Nevada region over ~60 years, including routine general-aviation losses. The specific 'Triangle' geometry and the claim that this is unusually high relative to comparable terrain/flight-density regions is disputed — NTSB data shows Sierra Nevada crash density is roughly in line with other high-terrain high-traffic corridors.

claims.text: The Bermuda Triangle has claimed about 50 ships and 20 planes over the past 60 years.

evidence.quote:
over the past 60 years, about 50
ships and 20 planes have mysteriously crashed

claims.rationale: Host uses these figures to argue the Nevada Triangle is more deadly. The Bermuda Triangle numbers are themselves disputed — Larry Kusche's 'The Bermuda Triangle Mystery—Solved' (1975) showed most cited cases were fabricated, occurred outside the triangle, or were ordinary losses. USCG and Lloyd's of London both state the region has no higher loss rate than comparable ocean areas. Numbers are impressionistic, not statistically meaningful.

claims.text: Record-setting aviator Steve Fossett disappeared on September 3, 2007 while flying a single-engine plane over Nevada's Great Basin Desert, and his plane wreckage was located about 65 miles from his takeoff point in 2008 with only two of his bones recovered.

evidence.quote: Steve Fossett, who vanished in 2007

Fossett flying his
single engine plane over Nevada's Great Basin
desert

the crash site was
located about sixty five miles from where Fossett
took off

Two bones were also found about
a half mile from the crash site.

claims.rationale: Well-documented: Fossett disappeared Sep 3 2007, declared dead Feb 2008, wreckage found Sep 29 2008 near Mammoth Lakes CA by hiker Preston Morrow. DNA-confirmed bone fragments recovered. Host's account matches NTSB report WPR08MA389 and contemporary NYT/AP coverage.

claims.text: Most of the land in the Nevada Triangle is owned by the U.S. government, and Area 51 sits inside the Triangle just northwest of Las Vegas.

evidence.quote:
Most of it belongs to
the United States government.

northwest of Vegas?
That's Area 51.

claims.rationale: Accurate: BLM, USFS, DOD, and Nellis Range Complex administer the bulk of Nevada's public land (~85% federal ownership statewide). Area 51 (Homey Airport/Groom Lake) is in southern Nevada ~83 miles NNW of Las Vegas, inside any reasonable Triangle boundary that includes Vegas as a vertex.

claims.text: The first Nevada Triangle aircraft disappearance was reported in 1938, the same year Area 51 began secret research operations.

evidence.quote: first report is nineteen thirty eight

They began doing research in
nineteen thirty eight they see.

claims.rationale: Area 51 did not exist in 1938. The Groom Lake site was first surveyed by the USAF/CIA in 1955 for U-2 testing, and the Nevada Test & Training Range was established in 1940. The 1938 claim appears to be a confabulation — possibly a transcript error or host misstatement. No publicly available record shows a 1938 Area 51 research program or a 1938 Nevada Triangle disappearance of note.

claims.text: In 1943, two B-24 bombers successively disappeared in the Sierra Nevada — the second while searching for the first — and the second was only found in 1955 when Huntington Lake was drained.

evidence.quote:
B twenty four bomber that
crashed in 1943.

Huntington
Lake Reservoir was drained for repairs to
the dam

claims.rationale: A B-24 did crash December 5, 1943 after taking off from Fresno/Hammer Field, and the wreckage was found in 1960 at Hester Lake. The Huntington Lake story likely conflates two separate B-24 incidents — the Huntington Lake 1955 recovery involves a different B-24 that crashed in October 1943. Host approximately correct on the broad strokes, off on specifics.

claims.text: Air Force Lieutenant David Steeves, pronounced dead after vanishing on May 9, 1957 in a T-33 trainer, reappeared 54 days later having survived in Kings Canyon National Park by fishing and hunting with his pistol.

evidence.quote:
David Steeves
was piloting a T-3 training jet

he reappeared
gaunt and dressed in tattered clothing

claims.rationale: Steeves's 1957 survival is documented in Time, Life, and Air Force accident files. Host says 'T-3' which is a transcript artifact — the actual aircraft was a T-33 Shooting Star. 54-day survival, Kings Canyon location, and the later vindication when Boy Scouts found the canopy (1977) are all accurate.

claims.text: Nevada Triangle disappearances are caused by secret prototype aircraft testing out of Area 51.

evidence.quote:
conspiracy theorists that claim the reason
so many flights have disappeared has
something to do with Area 51

claims.rationale: Host introduces this conspiracy framing explicitly to refute it via the 'geography and atmospheric conditions' alternative. Area 51 flight testing is real (U-2, A-12, SR-71, F-117, B-2 all tested there) but the 2,000-disappearance figure — even if accepted — includes decades of small-aircraft accidents across a huge area, not clustered around Groom Lake airspace. directTruth low: attribution of broad civilian aviation losses to secret-craft encounters has no supporting evidence.

claims.text: Most experts attribute Nevada Triangle crashes to mountain-wave turbulence where Sierra Nevada terrain running perpendicular to the jet stream creates violent downdrafts.

evidence.quote:
Sierra Nevada mountains run perpendicular
to the jet stream

most experts think the disappearances are
due to the geography and the atmospheric
conditions.

mountain wave, where planes are seemingly
ripped from the air

claims.rationale: Accurate characterization of the dominant meteorological explanation. Mountain-wave turbulence and lee-side downdrafts over the Sierra Nevada are well-documented in FAA weather advisories and NOAA mountain-wave forecasting. Fossett's case specifically has been analyzed (Vick 2009) as consistent with strong lee-side downdraft. This is the mainstream aviation-safety reading.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Offers the mundane meteorological cause for the crashes as alternative to the Area 51 conspiracy framing.

claims.text: Steve Fossett's aircraft, able to climb at only 500 fpm, was destroyed by a theorized 650-fpm downdraft which no aircraft in its class could have escaped.

evidence.quote:
some claim the area's climate
creates a special type of atmosphere, a
condition that can actually rip
the aircraft from the sky.

claims.rationale: The 650 fpm downdraft figure and Bellanca Decathlon's ~500 fpm climb ceiling are both reasonable; 'CPK' and 'copies' are transcript corruption of 'fpm'. Vick's 2009 analysis of Fossett's accident explicitly proposed lee-side downdraft. Whether that specific downdraft magnitude occurred has never been verified — NTSB did not ascribe a specific cause beyond 'in-flight collision with mountainous terrain'.

dependencies.rationale: Specific mechanism hypothesized for the Fossett crash covered in the earlier factual claim.
[supports] Specific application of the general mountain-wave explanation.

claims.text: The NTSB found no mechanical issues with Fossett's aircraft, ruling out equipment failure as the cause of the crash.

evidence.quote:
NTSB didn't find any mechanical
issues with the plane.

claims.rationale: NTSB accident report WPR08MA389 lists 'undetermined' as the probable cause, noting the aircraft impacted terrain during a steep descent. Engine and flight-control examinations did not reveal pre-impact mechanical failure. Host's claim matches the report.

claims.text: Rugged terrain and dense vegetation in the Sierra Nevada explain why so many crash sites are never found — the search for Fossett alone uncovered eight other previously-lost wrecks.

evidence.quote:
As for why so many of the crash
sites are never located, that's probably because of
the complex, rugged terrain

claims.rationale: The Fossett search did turn up additional pre-existing wrecks (widely reported in AP coverage Oct 2007). Terrain-and-vegetation concealment in the Sierra is a well-established factor in aviation search-and-rescue — CalMiss and Civil Air Patrol have documented multi-decade unrecovered wrecks. Host's framing is reasonable and supported.

claims.text: Major Ross Mulhare died July 11, 1986 flying an F-117 stealth fighter into a mountain near Bakersfield, and the cause has never been officially revealed.

evidence.quote: July 11th, 1986

Major Ross Mulheron flew
an F 117 into a mountain near Bakersfield

claims.rationale: Major Ross Mulhare did die in a F-117A crash July 11 1986 near Bakersfield CA. 'Mulheron' is a transcript error. The F-117 program was still classified, so specifics about the aircraft were publicly withheld; USAF attributed the crash to spatial disorientation after fatigue (declassified 1990s). 'Never officially revealed' overstates — cause was eventually released, just not immediately.
